title: Lets Stay Together

jokes.text: The writers put it on my door. I don't know what that's referring to.
jokes.setup: Liz claims not to know what the writers' joke refers to
jokes.punchline: She immediately explains exactly what it refers to in detail
jokes.explanation: Classic 30 Rock structure - character denies knowledge then immediately contradicts themselves. Well-executed misdirection.

jokes.text: Lez Lemon
jokes.setup: Writers making fun of Liz's name
jokes.punchline: Sexual wordplay on 'Liz Lemon'
jokes.explanation: Simple wordplay, economical but not particularly clever or original.

jokes.text: Winona Ryder in a hundred years
jokes.setup: Making fun of Liz's appearance
jokes.punchline: Comparing her to an aged version of a famous actress
jokes.explanation: Clever visual comparison with good economy. The specificity of 'hundred years' elevates it above generic aging jokes.

jokes.text: Fart Barfunkel
jokes.setup: Another name insult
jokes.punchline: Crude wordplay on Art Garfunkel
jokes.explanation: Juvenile toilet humor with perfect economy but relies on cheap laughs.

jokes.text: I don't get that, but it hurts
jokes.setup: Liz doesn't understand the Paul Simon reference
jokes.punchline: But acknowledges it's somehow hurtful anyway
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - captures Liz's neuroses and insecurity in an economical, relatable line.

jokes.text: To quote Rodney Dangerfield, 'Hey, I...'
jokes.setup: Liz begins to quote the famous comedian
jokes.punchline: Gets cut off before delivering the predictable punchline
jokes.explanation: Clever meta-joke about interrupting before the obvious punchline. Good structure and economy.

jokes.text: If you're about to say that you don't get any respect, you're right.
jokes.setup: Completing Liz's interrupted Rodney Dangerfield reference
jokes.punchline: Confirming she doesn't get respect in his typical blunt style
jokes.explanation: Good callback that completes the interrupted joke while maintaining Jack's character voice.

jokes.text: In a post-apocalyptic world, how would society even use you? Traveling bard. Radiation canary.
jokes.setup: Jack questioning Liz's value in extreme scenarios
jokes.punchline: Increasingly absurd and specific post-apocalyptic jobs
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist escalation. 'Radiation canary' is particularly inspired - both specific and darkly funny.

jokes.text: Will you ask congress where they put the USA network? I've been trying to find monk for, like, three months.
jokes.setup: Tracy making a request about congressional hearings
jokes.punchline: He thinks Congress controls TV scheduling and is personally invested in finding Monk reruns
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tracy moment - completely misunderstands how government/TV works while being oddly specific about Monk.

jokes.text: Imagine that your favorite corn chip manufacturer also owned the number one diarrhea medication. That'd be great, they could put a little sample of the medicine in each bag.
jokes.setup: Jack explaining vertical integration, Kenneth responds enthusiastically
jokes.punchline: Kenneth completely misses the problematic implications and sees it as helpful
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy - Kenneth's innocent optimism about clearly unethical business practices is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Keep thinking. Except then they might be tempted to make the corn chips give you...
jokes.setup: Jack starts to explain the obvious problem
jokes.punchline: Cuts himself off, letting the audience complete the thought
jokes.explanation: Good use of implication rather than stating the obvious. Trusts audience intelligence.

jokes.text: Do you know who gets elected to congress these days? Former athletes, washed-up actors, and women.
jokes.setup: Jack dismissing Congress members
jokes.punchline: Casual sexism delivered as if it's equivalent to the other categories
jokes.explanation: Sharp character moment showing Jack's casual privilege and prejudice. The escalation to 'women' is perfectly Jack.

jokes.text: If we have to stay and there aren't enough rooms, we have to share a room, I forgot to bring a shirt to sleep in and the stores are all closed...
jokes.setup: Liz panicking about practical concerns
jokes.punchline: Her neurotic overthinking of extremely unlikely scenarios
jokes.explanation: Classic Liz neurosis - overthinking mundane situations to absurd degrees. Very in character.

jokes.text: Jerry Bananaseed. And nothing else.
jokes.setup: Legal restrictions on using Johnny Appleseed
jokes.punchline: The only alternative is completely ridiculous and there's only one option
jokes.explanation: Absurdist legal comedy. The specificity and finality of 'and nothing else' makes it funnier.

jokes.text: We can't use Jerry Bananaseed. Someone with that name killed a bunch of nurses.
jokes.setup: The only legal name option seems viable
jokes.punchline: Even that ridiculous name is unusable for dark reasons
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation of the legal problems. The specificity of 'nurses' makes it darker and funnier.

jokes.text: I need you to get me something called vagitrax. It's for dry knees.
jokes.setup: Jenna asking Kenneth for help with medication
jokes.punchline: Mishears/misunderstands a clearly different medication and its purpose
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Jenna's vanity and medical ignorance. The misdirection works well.

jokes.text: Inside Schwartz 21, Hill street blues, wings is fun, Golden girls, quantum leap outsourced is the new friends
jokes.setup: A pageant contestant's talent portion
jokes.punchline: Rapid-fire TV show references that make no sense as a talent
jokes.explanation: Absurdist take on beauty pageant talents. The randomness and 'outsourced is the new friends' line elevate it.

jokes.text: That's like saying a guy is cool because he has just a speedboat.
jokes.setup: Kenneth dismisses pageants as 'just a pageant'
jokes.punchline: Jenna's comparison reveals her skewed values about what makes people impressive
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jenna moment - reveals her materialistic worldview where speedboats are obviously impressive.

jokes.text: There's three things standing between you and winning: Your breasts and wanting it bad enough.
jokes.setup: Jenna offering pageant wisdom
jokes.punchline: Math doesn't work (lists two things as three) and questionable advice about breasts
jokes.explanation: Classic Jenna - confidently wrong about basic math while giving inappropriate advice. Very in character.

jokes.text: My name is Rob Reiner. And before I became a congressman, I dabbled in television, won a couple of emmys.
jokes.setup: Rob Reiner introducing himself to NBC executives
jokes.punchline: Massively undersells his legendary Hollywood career as 'dabbling'
jokes.explanation: Good celebrity cameo joke - the understatement of calling his massive career 'dabbling' works well.

jokes.text: Say it, meathead.
jokes.setup: Rob Reiner about to say 'vertical integration'
jokes.punchline: Jack references Reiner's famous character from All in the Family
jokes.explanation: Good meta reference to Reiner's acting career. Economy is excellent but not particularly original.

jokes.text: I guess that's why big oil and Microsoft are such great American failures.
jokes.setup: Congressman argues vertical integration is bad for America
jokes.punchline: Jack sarcastically points out successful vertically integrated companies
jokes.explanation: Solid sarcastic rebuttal showing Jack's business acumen. Good structure and economy.

jokes.text: 1:32 p.m. Mark the time, ladies and gentlemen, that congress put a bullet in the head of the American farmer.
jokes.setup: Jack making a dramatic point about farming
jokes.punchline: Ridiculously over-the-top metaphor with specific time stamp for maximum drama
jokes.explanation: Great Jack moment - his tendency toward dramatic overstatement. The specific timestamp makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Why is NBC so racist?
jokes.setup: Jack thinks he's won the argument
jokes.punchline: Completely different accusation comes out of nowhere
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection and economy. Completely undercuts Jack's victory with brutal directness.

jokes.text: Oh, everybody born before Jesus is in hell, they went straight...
jokes.setup: Jenna trying to help Kenneth with pageant knowledge
jokes.punchline: Casual theological statement about pre-Christian damnation
jokes.explanation: Wonderfully absurd theological aside that's both dark and delivered casually by Jenna.

jokes.text: Just like Mickey Rourke did me to sexually.
jokes.setup: Jenna explaining reinvention process
jokes.punchline: Inappropriate sexual reference with awkward grammar
jokes.explanation: Very Jenna oversharing moment. The awkward phrasing 'did me to sexually' makes it more uncomfortable.

jokes.text: I don't know, ma'am. You made me think about it, and when I get to thinkin' 'bout it, it juh get worse.
jokes.setup: Jenna asks Kenneth to lose his accent
jokes.punchline: His accent gets comically thicker the more he tries to control it
jokes.explanation: Perfect Kenneth moment - his accent becomes incomprehensible when he tries to control it. Great escalation and character work.

jokes.text: From the hard-working moms and dads of Smithfield to those spoiled jags at brown to a thriving, flourishing Italian criminal community in Providence.
jokes.setup: Describing her diverse district
jokes.punchline: Casually includes organized crime as part of her constituency
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation that treats organized crime as just another demographic to serve. Good political satire.

jokes.text: Why is it that NBC looks about as diverse as a Wilco concert?
jokes.setup: Questioning NBC's diversity
jokes.punchline: Specific cultural reference that perfectly captures whiteness
jokes.explanation: Brilliant cultural reference - Wilco concerts are notoriously white audiences. Perfect specificity and economy.

jokes.text: Why did we cancel that? That doesn't make any sense.
jokes.setup: Jack mentioning Law & Order's long run
jokes.punchline: He immediately questions NBC's decision to cancel it
jokes.explanation: Good meta moment about real NBC decisions. Jack's momentary self-awareness is in character.

jokes.text: I don't really see color or gender, Mr. Chang.
jokes.setup: Congresswoman mentions being a black woman
jokes.punchline: Jack claims colorblindness while immediately misidentifying her ethnicity
jokes.explanation: Perfect satirical moment about colorblind racism. The immediate contradiction is expertly crafted and very Jack.

jokes.text: Rhubarb, rhubarb, rhubarb. Peas and carrots.
jokes.setup: Background conversation at NBC
jokes.punchline: Actors saying the standard background dialogue words rather than actual conversation
jokes.explanation: Meta joke about TV production. Shows they're breaking the fourth wall by having extras say their actual lines.

jokes.text: That's Tracy Jordan spelled backwards.
jokes.setup: Dotcom explaining his production company name
jokes.punchline: It's obviously not Tracy Jordan backwards at all
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist moment. The confidence with which he states something completely wrong is perfect.

jokes.text: Call Grizz. I need someone around who's not just a yes man. Whatever you say, Tray.
jokes.setup: Tracy wants someone who isn't a yes man
jokes.punchline: Dotcom immediately agrees like a yes man
jokes.explanation: Classic ironic structure - immediate contradiction of what was just requested. Good economy and timing.

jokes.text: The Chinese built the railroads, the Irish built and then filled the jails... A guy named Juan built my armoire.
jokes.setup: Jack celebrating American diversity with historical examples
jokes.punchline: Ends with casual stereotyping about his personal furniture maker
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jack moment showing his casual privilege and stereotyping disguised as appreciation for diversity.

jokes.text: I was too busy trying to remember the name of the black kid on community. D'nall glover.
jokes.setup: Jack couldn't focus on diversity issues
jokes.punchline: Was distracted by trying to remember an actor's name, which Liz mispronounces
jokes.explanation: Good character moments for both - Jack's scattered priorities and Liz's mispronunciation. Solid but not exceptional.

jokes.text: I may have lost my train of thought several minutes ago, but if I continue to talk like this, no one will notice, and when I stop, you will applaud my energy.
jokes.setup: Bookman giving a political speech
jokes.punchline: She openly admits to filibustering and explains the technique while doing it
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-commentary on political grandstanding. She's explaining the technique while demonstrating it.

jokes.text: Well, Lutz claims to be Inuit. At least that was his explanation when I found his poem about snow.
jokes.setup: Discussing diversity at TGS
jokes.punchline: Lutz's dubious claim to minority status based on questionable evidence
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist moment about fake diversity. The specificity of the snow poem makes it funnier.

jokes.text: So we know he's smart and superb at masturbation.
jokes.setup: Discussing Toofer's Harvard education
jokes.punchline: Liz makes an inappropriate assumption about Harvard students
jokes.explanation: Cringe humor that's somewhat cheap but delivered with Liz's characteristic awkwardness.

jokes.text: Monty Appleseed and I share a liquor locker at the opera.
jokes.setup: Legal problems with Appleseed name
jokes.punchline: Jack casually mentions knowing an Appleseed family member in typical elite fashion
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jack detail - of course he knows the litigious family socially. 'Liquor locker at the opera' is perfectly specific.

jokes.text: I forgot my doctor said no more frustrated noises, 'cause it makes my vocal cords go... Now it's gonna be like this all day.
jokes.setup: Liz mentions doctor's orders about frustrated noises
jokes.punchline: Immediately violates the rule while explaining it, causing the predicted problem
jokes.explanation: Great physical comedy setup - she causes the exact problem she's trying to avoid. Good escalation structure.

jokes.text: I wish you weren't such a Houston foreclosure of a human being.
jokes.setup: Jenna frustrated with Kenneth
jokes.punchline: Creative insult comparing him to a foreclosed house in a specific city
jokes.explanation: Brilliant creative insult. The specificity of 'Houston foreclosure' makes it both mean and absurdly poetic.

jokes.text: Why? It was a tentpole! A tentpole!
jokes.setup: Someone mentions Law & Order starting back up
jokes.punchline: Jack's passionate defense using industry jargon repeatedly
jokes.explanation: Good Jack moment showing his industry obsession. The repeated 'tentpole' shows his passion.

jokes.text: What if there was a talking dog? I'd like to see that incorporated into your rewrite.
jokes.setup: Dotcom pitches serious historical drama
jokes.punchline: Tracy wants to add a completely inappropriate talking dog
jokes.explanation: Classic Tracy moment - reducing serious historical drama to childish elements. Very in character.

jokes.text: Thank you, NBC. For all the laughs and tears. Once home to Kelsey Grammer, The golden girls, My name is Earl, The A-Team and cheers.
jokes.setup: Tribute song to NBC
jokes.punchline: Mix of actual NBC shows with shows that were never on NBC
jokes.explanation: Meta joke about TV network history confusion. Some shows listed were never on NBC, which is the joke.

jokes.text: It means the badger in Spanish. I'm not sure who did it.
jokes.setup: Someone asking about a sign
jokes.punchline: Random Spanish animal fact delivered as if it's relevant information
jokes.explanation: Absurdist non-sequitur. The randomness and confidence make it mildly funny but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Oh, God. Tejon face. Is this about the TV interview?
jokes.setup: Liz seeing Toofer's expression
jokes.punchline: She knows his facial expression well enough to have named it
jokes.explanation: Good character detail - Liz knows Toofer well enough to recognize and name his expressions.

jokes.text: Aqui viene el tejon!
jokes.setup: Continuation of the badger/tejon running gag
jokes.punchline: Now they're announcing the badger's arrival in Spanish
jokes.explanation: Running gag escalation. The commitment to the absurd badger theme is mildly amusing.

jokes.text: Good. And there's a lot of buzz. Can you hear it too? Or is my tinnitus acting up?
jokes.setup: Tracy saying there's buzz about his show
jokes.punchline: He thinks 'buzz' might be literal ringing in his ears
jokes.explanation: Good Tracy moment - takes metaphor literally in a way that reveals health issues. Classic character confusion.

jokes.text: But they don't have women's sports bars. Men can have cramping too, you know.
jokes.setup: Food segregation between TGS and Tracy's production
jokes.punchline: Weird complaint about gendered energy bars and male medical needs
jokes.explanation: Mildly absurdist complaint about energy bar gender marketing. Somewhat forced but has its moments.

jokes.text: Magnolia bakery? Oh, sorry, hips.
jokes.setup: Bookman mentioning Sex and the City tour
jokes.punchline: Apologizes to her hips for even mentioning the bakery
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing her diet consciousness and relationship with her body. Economical delivery.

jokes.text: The bridge was supposed to be shuffle-ball-change, maxi Ford, Cincinnati, Cincinnati, double-time shim-sham-shimmy, toe punch.
jokes.setup: Jenna critiquing Kenneth's pageant performance
jokes.punchline: Extremely specific and absurd dance terminology delivered with authority
jokes.explanation: Good Jenna expertise moment. The specificity of made-up dance terms shows her pageant background.

jokes.text: If I hadn't seen you come out of me in that parking lot, I'd swear you weren't my daughter.
jokes.setup: Mother's disappointment in pageant performance
jokes.punchline: Reveals Jenna was born in a parking lot while expressing disappointment
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark character detail. The casual mention of parking lot birth while expressing disappointment is perfectly Jenna's origin story.

jokes.text: Or my name isn't Yustrepa Gronkowitz. I mean, Jenna Maroney.
jokes.setup: Jenna making a dramatic promise
jokes.punchline: Accidentally reveals her real ethnic name before correcting to stage name
jokes.explanation: Excellent character reveal. The accidental slip shows Jenna's invented persona and the name is perfectly absurd.

jokes.text: I would argue that TV is more of a boys club than a white club.
jokes.setup: Discussion about diversity in TV
jokes.punchline: Liz tries to redirect focus to gender issues and diminishes racial concerns
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Liz's problematic attempt to center herself in diversity discussions.

jokes.text: I like your dress. Do you have to wear a bra with it?
jokes.setup: TV host trying to be friendly to Liz
jokes.punchline: Completely inappropriate personal question about undergarments
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment. The inappropriateness of the question on TV is perfectly awkward.

jokes.text: That's me in college. That's not great.
jokes.setup: Liz seeing a picture of black Jesus
jokes.punchline: She identifies it as herself in college, then realizes how bad that sounds
jokes.explanation: Good self-aware cringe moment. Liz realizes how her comment sounds and the immediate regret is perfect.

jokes.text: But, sir, I was born in Palo Alto.
jokes.setup: Jack dismissing Jonathan
jokes.punchline: Jonathan's irrelevant geographic fact as defense
jokes.explanation: Good Jonathan character moment - his non-sequitur defense is perfectly in character for his oddness.

jokes.text: I'd like to tell you that we filled the minority slot in the NBC page program with a native American fellow named wants to get sandwiches, but you made me hire that guy instead.
jokes.setup: Jeffrey discussing diversity hiring
jokes.punchline: Reveals absurd Native American name and casual racism about 'that guy'
jokes.explanation: Dark satire of tokenism and casual racism in corporate hiring. The absurd name is funny but problematic.

jokes.text: That young man is a hillbilly with a girl's middle name and, because his county never rejoined the union, a foreigner.
jokes.setup: Criticizing Kenneth as a diversity hire
jokes.punchline: Increasingly specific and absurd reasons why Kenneth counts as diverse
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation of Kenneth's outsider status. The Civil War reference is particularly inspired and specific.

jokes.text: Now you're untied? Have you two been talking?
jokes.setup: Kenneth notices someone's shoelaces
jokes.punchline: He thinks untied shoes are evidence of conspiracy
jokes.explanation: Perfect Kenneth paranoia moment. His innocent confusion about shoelaces as communication is beautifully absurd.

jokes.text: Tell me, you obviously lonely and tired woman.
jokes.setup: Congresswoman addressing Liz
jokes.punchline: Brutal but accurate assessment delivered casually
jokes.explanation: Brutal but economical character assessment. The directness and accuracy make it both funny and painful.

jokes.text: This country was founded on certain principles. Freedom, troops, America, flag. Now I might not ow where I'm going with this, but I know we will get there together!
jokes.setup: Congresswoman beginning patriotic speech
jokes.punchline: Lists nonsensical 'principles' then admits she has no point
jokes.explanation: Excellent satire of empty political rhetoric. 'Troops' and 'flag' as founding principles is perfectly absurd.

jokes.text: There are thousands of jobs at stake, hundreds of second homes, and your ridiculous grandstanding could ruin the whole thing, like luffing your spinnaker during a yachting regatta.
jokes.setup: Jack explaining stakes of the deal
jokes.punchline: Priorities reveal privilege, then uses completely elitist sailing metaphor
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jack moment - his priorities (second homes) and sailing metaphor show complete disconnect from normal people.

jokes.text: Yes, maybe I get carried away sometimes with my love for this great country and the troops and the flagtroops...
jokes.setup: Bookman defending her patriotic rhetoric
jokes.punchline: Accidentally combines 'flag' and 'troops' into nonsense word
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the patriotic rhetoric parody. 'Flagtroops' is a perfect malapropism.

jokes.text: The first generation works their fingers to the bone making things. The next generation goes to college, innovates new ideas. The third generation... snowboards and takes improv classes.
jokes.setup: Jack explaining immigration cycles
jokes.punchline: Third generation completely abandons work ethic for leisure activities
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation structure. The specificity of 'snowboards and takes improv classes' perfectly captures privileged aimlessness.

jokes.text: I'm cutting that fat cracker's head off. I'm part eskimo. Hate crime!
jokes.setup: Lutz hearing threats
jokes.punchline: Violent threat followed by claiming minority status for hate crime protection
jokes.explanation: Good callback to Lutz's dubious minority claims. The escalation from violence to victimhood is absurdist.

jokes.text: We were here first!
jokes.setup: Food territory conflict
jokes.punchline: Treating craft services like indigenous land rights
jokes.explanation: Absurdist escalation of the food conflict. The indigenous rights reference is somewhat forced but economical.

jokes.text: You think you got it hard, I've got two dates tonight. Stanley, even for a dog, you are a dog.
jokes.setup: Character complaining about dating problems
jokes.punchline: Talking dog makes pun on his species and behavior
jokes.explanation: Predictable talking dog humor. The pun on 'dog' works but isn't particularly clever or original.

jokes.text: Ain't life a bitch?
jokes.setup: Philosophical comment about life
jokes.punchline: Dog character making species-appropriate pun
jokes.explanation: Very predictable dog pun. Expected and not particularly clever, though economically delivered.

tmdb_overview: Jack must appear before Congress to discuss NBC's merger with KableTown. He hopes that he will charm celebrity members, but one Congresswoman, Regina Bookman, demands more diversity in its programming lineup. Jack quickly enlists Tracy to come up with some new development ideas. Meanwhile, Liz is fed up with the flack her writing staff gives her, and Jenna helps Kenneth reapply to the extremely competitive Page Program.
insight: Five-minute cold open escalates from wedding planning to pure absurdist chaos.
